findings: Species identity is supported: the plate depicts a Black-and-white Warbler with bold black-and-white striping, a white crown stripe, strong facial pattern, a thin slightly downcurved bill, two broad white wing bars, and a short dark tail marked white on the outer feathers.
The displayed common name, scientific name, family, all three measurements, behavior statement, and habitat statement agree with the independently opened Cornell and Audubon species pages.

The side-profile bird is anatomically coherent: one head, one beak, two legs, one tail, and a visible near wing with the far wing naturally occluded. Its stance, longish slightly downcurved bill, short-necked/streamlined head, short tail, and bark-foraging perch are appropriate. Feet and toes are somewhat stylized and oversized but not materially incorrect.

The illustration reads as an adult male due to its strongly black cheek and heavily streaked underparts. The black-spotted undertail coverts are only partly visible, so that field mark is not especially clear in the main figure.
No place name, ZIP code, coordinates, map, or local-observation detail appears on the candidate plate.

verification_sources.title: Black-and-white Warbler Identification — All About Birds
